abstract: LLMs have shown strong potential to advance scientific discovery. Whether they possess the capacity for foundational innovation, however, remains an open question. In this work, we focus on a prerequisite for foundational innovation: can LLMs reinvent foundational algorithms in computer science? Our \textit{Unlearn-and-Reinvent} pipeline applies LLM unlearning to remove a specific foundational algorithm, such as Dijkstra's or Euclid's algorithm, from an LLM's pretrained knowledge, and then tests whether the model can reinvent it in a controlled environment. To enable effective unlearning, we adopt a GRPO-based, on-policy unlearning method. Across 10 target algorithms, 3 strong open-weight models, and 3 hint levels, our experiments demonstrate that (1) the strongest model Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 successfully reinvents 50% of the algorithms with no hint, 70% at hint level 1, and 90% at hint level 2; (2) a few high-level hints can enhance the reinvention success rate, but even step-by-step hints fail for those complicated algorithms; and (3) test-time reinforcement learning enables successful reinvention for the Strassen algorithm at hint level 2. Through analyses of output trajectories and ablation studies, we find that generative verifier in the reinvention phase plays a critical role in sustaining models' reasoning strength, helping to avoid the ``thought collapse'' phenomenon. These findings offer insights into both the potential and current limits of LLMs' innovative thinking.

citations.fullCitation: Bernardino Romera-Paredes, Mohammadamin Barekatain, Alexander Novikov, Matej Balog, M. Pawan Kumar, Emilien Dupont, Francisco J. R. Ruiz, Jordan Ellenberg, Pengming Wang, Omar Fawzi, Pushmeet Kohli, and Alhussein Fawzi. Mathematical discoveries from program search with large language models. Nature, 2023. doi: 10.1038/ s41586-023-06924-6.
citations.justification: This paper, which introduces the FunSearch system, is cited in the introduction to establish the state-of-the-art in using LLMs for algorithmic discovery. It provides the essential context and motivation for the main paper's central question of whether LLMs can produce foundational discoveries rather than just incremental improvements.
citations.title: Mathematical discoveries from program search with large language models

citations.fullCitation: Yinzhi Cao and Junfeng Yang. Towards making systems forget with machine unlearning. In 2015 IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy, pp. 463–480, 2015. doi: 10.1109/SP.2015.35.
citations.justification: This is a foundational citation for the concept of "machine unlearning," which is the core technique enabling the paper's 'Unlearn-and-Reinvent' pipeline. The entire experimental methodology is predicated on the ability to make a model forget specific knowledge, a concept pioneered by this work.
citations.title: Towards making systems forget with machine unlearning

citations.fullCitation: Zhihong Shao, Peiyi Wang, Qihao Zhu, Runxin Xu, Junxiao Song, Xiao Bi, Haowei Zhang, Mingchuan Zhang, Y. K. Li, Y. Wu, and Daya Guo. Deepseekmath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models, 2024. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/ 2402.03300.
citations.justification: This paper is cited as the source for Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), the specific on-policy unlearning method adopted by the authors. This citation is crucial as the paper's methodology section is built around this GRPO-based approach, which they find to be highly effective for their experiments.
citations.title: Deepseekmath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models

citations.fullCitation: Lunjun Zhang, Arian Hosseini, Hritik Bansal, Mehran Kazemi, Aviral Kumar, and Rishabh Agarwal. Generative verifiers: Reward modeling as next-token prediction, 2025b. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.15240.
citations.justification: This work is cited for the "generative verifier," a key component of the paper's reinvention phase. The authors dedicate a significant ablation study to demonstrate that this verifier plays a critical role in preventing "thought collapse" and sustaining the model's reasoning, making it central to their findings and conclusions.
citations.title: Generative verifiers: Reward modeling as next-token prediction

intermediateReport:
## Research Paper Report: Can Large Language Models Reinvent Foundational Algorithms?

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by Jian Zhao\* (Xiongan AI Institute, Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications), Haoren Luo\* (Institute for Interdisciplinary Information Sciences, Tsinghua University), Yu Wang (Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences), Yuhan Cao (Independent Researcher), Pingyue Sheng (Institute for Interdisciplinary Information Sciences, Tsinghua University), and Tianxing He\*† (Xiongan AI Institute, Institute for Interdisciplinary Information Sciences at Tsinghua University, Shanghai Qi Zhi Institute). (\* denotes equal contribution, † denotes corresponding author).

### 2. Broader Research Landscape

The work addresses a developing area in Artificial Intelligence concerning the advanced capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) in scientific discovery and problem-solving. Recent advancements have demonstrated LLMs' potential to generate algorithms that, in some cases, surpass human-designed methods, as exemplified by systems like FunSearch and AlphaEvolve. However, the existing research landscape contains an open question regarding whether LLMs possess the capacity for foundational scientific discovery, which involves inventing entirely new concepts, rather than primarily producing incremental improvements or adapting existing knowledge.

This paper positions itself within this context by focusing on a specific aspect of foundational innovation: the reinvention of foundational algorithms in computer science. Unlike previous work that might allow LLMs to leverage their full pre-trained knowledge, this study introduces an "Unlearn-and-Reinvent" pipeline. This pipeline directly challenges LLMs to reconstruct core algorithmic knowledge after it has been explicitly removed, thereby aiming to evaluate their intrinsic capacity for algorithmic invention rather than mere retrieval or minor modification of remembered information. The methodology also incorporates aspects of LLM unlearning, a field focused on selectively removing specific knowledge from pre-trained models while preserving general utility, and AI-driven research, which explores combining language models with program search and reinforcement learning for discovery.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to evaluate whether Large Language Models possess the capacity for foundational innovation by attempting to reinvent foundational algorithms in computer science. The authors define foundational innovation as the creation of groundbreaking contributions that form the basis of a field, rather than solely incremental improvements.

The motivation for this objective stems from several observations and challenges:
*   **Advancements in LLM capabilities:** Recent LLMs have exhibited enhanced reasoning and agentic capabilities, leading to successes in complex problem-solving, including algorithmic discovery. This prompts the question of whether these capabilities extend to true foundational invention.
*   **Distinguishing invention from memorization:** Current LLMs are trained on vast datasets that inherently contain knowledge of foundational algorithms like Dijkstra's or Euclid's. This makes it difficult to ascertain whether a model "invents" an algorithm or merely recalls it from its training data.
*   **Computational cost of retraining:** A theoretically ideal way to test pure invention would be to train an LLM from scratch on data that explicitly excludes the target algorithm. However, the computational resources required for such large-scale retraining are prohibitive.
*   **Need for a controlled experimental setup:** To overcome the aforementioned challenges, the researchers sought a computationally efficient and controlled method to simulate a scenario where an LLM has no prior *explicit* knowledge of a specific algorithm and must therefore reinvent it.

To address these motivations, the study proposes the "Unlearn-and-Reinvent" pipeline. This pipeline leverages LLM unlearning techniques to remove specific algorithmic knowledge (e.g., Dijkstra's algorithm) from a pre-trained model. Subsequently, the unlearned model is tasked with reinventing that specific algorithm, allowing for a more direct assessment of its innovative capacity in a controlled environment. The research also aims to investigate the factors that influence reinvention success, such as the level of external hints and the role of interactive feedback mechanisms.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The research employs an "Unlearn-and-Reinvent" pipeline, structured into two main phases: the unlearning phase and the reinvention phase.

**4.1 The Unlearning Phase**
The goal of this phase is to remove specific foundational algorithms from the LLM's pre-trained knowledge while maintaining its general utility.
*   **Target Algorithms:** Ten foundational algorithms across various computer science domains were selected: Dijkstra, Floyd-Warshall, Bellman-Ford, Prim, Euclidean, KMP, Manacher, Moore Vote, Gray, and Strassen.
*   **Unlearning Method:** A Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)-based, on-policy unlearning method was adopted, integrated with a cold start stage.
    *   **GRPO Objective:** The unlearning process optimizes a GRPO-style objective that balances forgetting (increasing the language modeling loss on a "forget set" $D_{forget}$) and utility preservation (minimizing KL divergence to a reference policy and optimizing on a "retain set" $D_{retain}$).
    *   **Forget Set ($D_{forget}$):** Consists of queries related to the target algorithm, designed to break two associations: algorithm-to-context (mentioning algorithm does not recall details) and context-to-algorithm (problem description does not recall algorithm name).
    *   **Retain Set ($D_{retain}$):** Contains non-target examples, sampled from general post-training datasets, to preserve the model's general capabilities and response style.
    *   **Reward Function:** For each sampled response to a forget query, an LLM-as-a-judge assigns a binary reward of 1 only if the response meets three criteria: 1) no target knowledge disclosure ($k_j=0$), 2) no algorithm-name corruption ($c_j=0$), and 3) readability ($u_j=1$). This multi-dimensional reward prevents reward hacking (e.g., hallucinating names or generating gibberish).
    *   **Cold Start Stage:** To address the issue of zero rewards in early unlearning attempts, an initialization set ($D_{init}$) of target-related queries paired with refusal-style responses (generated by the base model with target names replaced by random tokens and then restored) is used for supervised fine-tuning. This guides the model toward refusal-style behavior on forget queries.

**4.2 The Reinvention Phase**
This phase evaluates the unlearned model's ability to independently reinvent the forgotten algorithm.
*   **Interactive Environment:** The unlearned model interacts with a Python interpreter in a coding task. For each target algorithm, a programming task is defined with a prompt ($I_g$), a test suite ($T_g$), and runtime/memory limits ($\tau_g$, $\mu_g$). The prompt describes the computational problem without naming the algorithm.
*   **Success Criteria:** A submission is successful if it passes all tests in $T_g$ within the specified runtime and memory limits, which are calibrated to the complexity of the target algorithm.
*   **Generative Verifier:** For failed submissions, a generative verifier, instantiated by the unlearned model itself, provides diagnostic natural language feedback. This feedback helps the model identify errors and revise its approach, aiming to guide sustained exploration rather than premature abandonment.
*   **Hierarchical Prompt Levels:** To assess the impact of external guidance, three hint levels are employed:
    *   **No Hint:** Only the task description is provided.
    *   **Level 1 (High-level Hint):** A conceptual hint about the algorithmic approach (e.g., "greedy concept" for Dijkstra).
    *   **Level 2 (Step-by-step Hint):** A detailed explanation of the algorithm in natural language, providing explicit steps and sub-steps.
*   **Test-time Reinforcement Learning (RL):** For specific algorithm-hint pairs with zero initial reinvention success, test-time RL is applied. The model is optimized on the test problem itself using a continuous reward of $1/T$ for correct solutions (where T is running time) and zero for incorrect ones. This encourages correct and efficient solutions during inference-time optimization.

**4.3 Models and Evaluation:**
*   **Models:** Three open-weight models were used: Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, and Ministral-3-14B-Reasoning-2512.
*   **Evaluation Metrics:**
    *   **Forgetting Rate (FR):** Measures unlearning efficacy using a probe set of multiple-choice and open-ended questions. A judge model assigns 1 if no target knowledge is revealed.
    *   **Utility Preservation:** Evaluated on LiveCodeBench, AIME25, and BFCL-v3 benchmarks.
    *   **Reinvention Success Rate (RSR):** The fraction of successful attempts for a target algorithm, with 8 problem variants and 128 trials per prompt level.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research yielded several findings regarding LLMs' ability to reinvent foundational algorithms:

*   **Algorithmic Reinvention Capabilities:** The unlearned LLMs demonstrated varying degrees of success in reinventing algorithms. The strongest model, Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, successfully reinvented 50% of the 10 target algorithms with no hint, achieving an average Reinvention Success Rate (RSR) of 21.8%. Algorithms like Gray code generation and Euclidean algorithm, which have problem statements that more directly constrain the solution space, were more frequently reinvented without hints. In contrast, algorithms requiring non-obvious data structures or counterintuitive invariants (e.g., KMP, Manacher, Strassen) remained challenging, often unsolved across all models with no hint.

*   **Impact of Hints:** External hints consistently improved reinvention performance across all three models. With high-level hints (Level 1), Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507's RSR increased to 48.5%, and with step-by-step hints (Level 2), it further rose to 80.9%. This indicates that hints can compensate for removed knowledge. However, even with detailed step-by-step hints, the most complex algorithms (KMP and Strassen) often remained unsolved, suggesting that hints improve performance for moderately difficult algorithms but do not fully overcome the reasoning challenges posed by the hardest ones.

*   **Effectiveness of Test-time Reinforcement Learning (RL):** Test-time RL was applied to algorithm-hint pairs where the initial RSR was 0. This approach demonstrated an ability to shift the model towards correct and more efficient solutions. Notably, test-time RL enabled the successful reinvention of Strassen's algorithm at hint Level 2 for Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, achieving a 62.5% success rate for correct outputs. For other algorithms like KMP (no hint) and Prim (Level 1), test-time RL also improved solution efficiency (e.g., KMP improved execution time from 2.12s to 1.80s) even if it did not lead to full reinvention from zero success.

*   **Role of the Generative Verifier:** Ablation studies on the generative verifier revealed its critical role in sustaining model reasoning. Without verifier feedback, models exhibited a "thought collapse" phenomenon, characterized by a progressive decline in the number of reasoning tokens generated over interaction rounds. This indicated reduced exploration and premature abandonment of problem-solving. Both the default self-verifier and a stronger "oracle" verifier (DeepSeek-V3.2) mitigated this collapse, maintaining longer outputs and more sustained exploration. The presence of verifier feedback also led to higher reinvention success rates (e.g., Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 RSR increased from 9.5% without verifier to 21.8% with self-verifier and 34.8% with oracle verifier for no-hint scenarios).

*   **Unlearning Effectiveness and Robustness:** The unlearning procedure achieved near-complete forgetting of target algorithms (average Forgetting Rate (FR) between 96.0% and 100.0% across models). Crucially, this was accomplished while largely preserving the models' general utility on standard benchmarks (LiveCodeBench, AIME25, BFCL), with utility metrics remaining stable relative to the original models. Furthermore, reinvention findings were robust to post-unlearning distillation, indicating the stability of the unlearning effects. A comparison with other on-policy unlearning methods (NPO, DPO, GradAscent) showed that GRPO achieved the highest average utility preservation (42.7% LCB) while maintaining 100% FR.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The findings of this research offer insights into both the potential and current limitations of LLMs' innovative thinking, particularly regarding foundational algorithmic discovery.

*   **Evidence for Algorithmic Invention Beyond Memorization:** The successful reinvention of several foundational algorithms without hints, following explicit unlearning of prior knowledge, suggests that LLMs possess a genuine capacity for algorithmic invention. This indicates that their problem-solving goes beyond mere retrieval or incremental modification of memorized solutions, hinting at an underlying ability to explore solution spaces and construct novel approaches. This is a significant step in understanding the generative capabilities of LLMs in structured domains.

*   **Identification of Algorithmic Complexity Boundaries:** The clear distinction between algorithms that could be reinvented and those that remained unsolved (e.g., KMP, Strassen) highlights a structural boundary in current LLMs' innovative capabilities. LLMs appear to excel when the solution path is reachable through incremental search or greedy approaches but struggle with algorithms requiring non-intuitive design choices, complex invariants, or significant "leaps" in conceptual understanding. This provides a direction for future research, indicating where LLMs currently lack human-like ingenuity.

*   **Importance of Interactive Feedback and Learning at Inference Time:** The mitigating effect of the generative verifier against "thought collapse" underscores the importance of explicit, diagnostic feedback for sustaining an LLM's reasoning and exploratory behavior over multiple interaction rounds. Furthermore, the success of test-time reinforcement learning, particularly in enabling the reinvention of Strassen's algorithm, suggests that inference-time optimization can complement static models by amplifying exploratory signals and improving correctness and efficiency. This indicates that dynamic, adaptive learning during the problem-solving process is a crucial component for maximizing LLMs' discovery potential.

*   **Advancements in LLM Unlearning:** The effective implementation of the GRPO-based unlearning pipeline, achieving near-complete forgetting while preserving general utility, contributes to the field of LLM unlearning. This methodology provides a robust tool for controlled experiments that probe the intrinsic knowledge and reasoning abilities of LLMs, enabling more accurate assessments of their generative capacities.

*   **Implications for AI-Driven Research:** The work contributes to the broader field of AI-driven research by setting up a framework for evaluating foundational discovery. While acknowledging the limitation of post hoc unlearning (not guaranteeing complete knowledge erasure), the study's results still provide a strong empirical basis for discussing the frontiers of LLM innovation. Future work may extend this pipeline to more open-ended scientific discovery settings beyond foundational algorithms, potentially leading to AI systems that can genuinely invent new scientific laws, theorems, or hypotheses.
overview:
The question of whether Large Language Models (LLMs) can generate truly foundational scientific discoveries or if they are primarily sophisticated retrieval engines remains a central debate in artificial intelligence. While LLMs have demonstrated the ability to optimize existing code or solve competitive programming problems, these successes often draw upon a vast training corpus that already contains the solutions. To determine if a model can "invent" a concept rather than just "remember" it, researchers have introduced a methodology known as the "Unlearn-and-Reinvent" pipeline.

This approach involves two distinct steps: first, the model is subjected to a targeted unlearning process to remove specific algorithmic knowledge. Second, the model is tasked with solving a problem that necessitates the reinvention of that forgotten algorithm. By removing the model's access to the "answer key" provided during pre-training, researchers can more accurately assess its intrinsic capacity for algorithmic reasoning and innovation.

![Figure 1: Overview of the Unlearn-and-Reinvent pipeline, illustrating the transition from a model containing algorithmic knowledge to one that must reconstruct it through iterative reasoning and feedback.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05716v1/x1.png)

## The Unlearning Phase: Removing Specific Knowledge

To ensure that the reinvention process is authentic, the pipeline begins by purging the model of ten foundational algorithms: Dijkstra, Floyd-Warshall, Bellman-Ford, Prim, Euclidean, KMP, Manacher, Moore Vote, Gray, and Strassen. This is not a simple deletion but a complex fine-tuning process designed to break the associations between problem descriptions and their corresponding algorithmic solutions.

The researchers employed a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)-based unlearning method. This technique optimizes the model to forget specific information while preserving its general utility on unrelated tasks. The unlearning objective balances three components:
1.  **Forgetting:** Increasing the loss on a "forget set" $D_{\text{forget}}$, which contains queries about the target algorithms.
2.  **Utility Preservation:** Minimizing the divergence from the original model's behavior on a "retain set" $D_{\text{retain}}$ of general knowledge.
3.  **Reward Function:** An LLM-as-a-judge evaluates the model's responses during unlearning. A reward is granted only if the response contains no target knowledge ($k_j = 0$), does not corrupt the algorithm's name ($c_j = 0$), and remains readable ($u_j = 1$).

To prevent the model from generating gibberish or simply refusing to answer any questions, a "cold start" stage is used. In this stage, the model is fine-tuned on an initialization set $D_{\text{init}}$ that pairs algorithm-related queries with polite refusals or alternative (non-target) explanations. This guides the model toward a state where it "knows" it should not provide the specific algorithm without losing its ability to communicate effectively.

## The Reinvention Challenge: Discovery Through Interaction

Once the model has successfully "forgotten" the target algorithms—verified by a near-100% forgetting rate on multiple-choice and open-ended probes—it enters the reinvention phase. In this phase, the model is presented with a programming task described in natural language without using the name of the algorithm. For instance, instead of being asked to "implement Dijkstra's algorithm," the model is asked to "invent a single-source shortest path algorithm for non-negative graphs with a time complexity of $O(N^2)$."

The reinvention environment is interactive, consisting of:
*   **The Problem Instance:** Defined by a prompt $I_g$, a test suite $T_g$, and specific resource constraints like runtime $\tau_g$ and memory $\mu_g$.
*   **A Python Interpreter:** The model generates code and receives immediate execution results.
*   **A Generative Verifier:** When a submission fails (e.g., due to a timeout or incorrect output), the model generates its own diagnostic feedback to guide its next attempt.

The difficulty of the task is modulated using three hint levels. Level 0 (No Hint) provides only the task description. Level 1 provides a high-level conceptual hint, such as suggesting a "greedy approach." Level 2 provides a detailed, step-by-step natural language explanation of the algorithm's logic.

![Figure 2: Example of the interactive reinvention process for the single-source shortest path problem. The model iterates through multiple rounds of coding and receives feedback from the verifier until it either succeeds or reaches the round limit.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05716v1/x2.png)

## Findings on Algorithmic Reinvention

The study found that LLMs, particularly those optimized for reasoning like Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, are indeed capable of reinventing several foundational algorithms. Without any hints, the strongest models achieved a Reinvention Success Rate (RSR) of 21.8% across the ten algorithms. 

However, success varied significantly depending on the nature of the algorithm. Algorithms that could be reached through intuitive leaps or incremental improvements were more likely to be reinvented. For example:
*   **Euclidean Algorithm and Gray Code:** These were frequently reinvented without hints because their problem constraints strongly guide the search space toward the correct solution.
*   **Dijkstra and Prim:** These often required at least a high-level hint to steer the model away from less efficient approaches like Bellman-Ford.
*   **KMP and Strassen:** These remained extremely difficult. Algorithms requiring non-obvious data structures (like the prefix function in KMP) or counterintuitive mathematical transformations (like the specific sub-matrix multiplications in Strassen) were rarely reinvented without significant external guidance.

The presence of hints dramatically improved performance. With step-by-step hints (Level 2), the RSR for the strongest model jumped to 80.9%, demonstrating that the models could effectively translate natural language logic into functional code even for algorithms they had previously "forgotten."

## The "Thought Collapse" Phenomenon and the Role of Verifiers

A critical discovery during the reinvention phase was the phenomenon of "thought collapse." In interactive settings without external feedback, models often showed a progressive decline in the length and quality of their reasoning. As the number of rounds increased, the models generated fewer and fewer "thinking" tokens, eventually stagnating or producing repetitive, low-effort solutions.

![Figure 4: Illustration of thought collapse, where the number of output tokens decreases significantly over multiple rounds of interaction.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05716v1/x4.png)

To combat this, the researchers utilized a generative verifier. This verifier acts as a self-critique mechanism, where the model analyzes why its previous code failed (e.g., "the complexity is $O(N^3)$ because of the nested loop, but the requirement is $O(N^2)$") and suggests specific improvements. The inclusion of this feedback loop was found to be essential for maintaining exploration.

![Figure 5: The impact of different verifiers on output length. The presence of a verifier (self or oracle) significantly mitigates the collapse of reasoning tokens compared to a scenario with no verifier.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05716v1/x5.png)

By providing diagnostic feedback, the verifier allowed the model to maintain a consistent "thought volume" across dozens of rounds. This sustained exploration was often the difference between success and failure for more complex tasks like Dijkstra's algorithm.

## Pushing the Frontier with Test-Time RL

For the most difficult algorithms where the initial success rate was zero, the researchers applied test-time reinforcement learning (RL). Unlike traditional training, test-time RL optimizes the model on a specific problem during the inference phase. The model is rewarded based on the correctness and efficiency of its generated code, with a reward signal defined as $1/T$ for correct solutions, where $T$ is the execution time.

This approach proved particularly effective for Strassen's matrix multiplication algorithm. While the unlearned model initially failed to produce a correct implementation even with Level 2 hints, test-time RL allowed it to refine its search until it achieved a 62.5% success rate. This suggests that even when a model cannot immediately "reason" its way to a foundational discovery, the combination of guided exploration and performance-based rewards can push it across the finish line.

![Figure 6: Reward curves during test-time reinforcement learning for difficult algorithms. The star indicates a successful reinvention of Strassen's algorithm after several steps of optimization.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.05716v1/x6.png)

## Implications for AI-Driven Research

The "Unlearn-and-Reinvent" methodology provides a rigorous framework for distinguishing between an LLM's ability to recall information and its ability to innovate. The results suggest that while current LLMs possess a genuine capacity for algorithmic invention, they are bounded by the complexity of the "conceptual leap" required. 

Algorithms that require highly specific, non-intuitive invariants remain at the edge of current capabilities. However, the study also highlights that interactive environments, self-verification, and inference-time optimization are powerful tools that can extend the reach of these models. This research lays the groundwork for future AI systems that might not only reinvent known foundational algorithms but eventually discover entirely new ones that have yet to be conceived by human researchers.

keyInsights: LLMs possess a genuine capacity for algorithmic invention, as evidenced by successfully reinventing foundational algorithms after their knowledge was explicitly unlearned.

The ability of LLMs to invent new algorithms is bounded by complexity, performing better with algorithms reachable via incremental search but struggling with those requiring non-intuitive design or complex invariants.

Interactive diagnostic feedback from a generative verifier and test-time reinforcement learning are crucial for sustaining LLM reasoning, preventing "thought collapse," and enabling discovery of complex or efficient solutions.
originalProblem: It is challenging to distinguish if Large Language Models (LLMs) truly invent algorithms or merely recall them from vast training data, which inherently contains knowledge of foundational algorithms.
Retraining LLMs from scratch on data explicitly excluding target algorithms is computationally prohibitive for testing pure invention.
A controlled and computationally efficient experimental setup is needed to assess LLMs' intrinsic capacity for foundational algorithmic discovery.
results: The best-performing unlearned model, Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, reinvented 50% of the 10 target algorithms with no hints, achieving an average Reinvention Success Rate (RSR) of 21.8%.
External hints significantly improved reinvention, with high-level hints increasing RSR to 48.5% and step-by-step hints raising it to 80.9% for the top model.

Test-time reinforcement learning enabled the reinvention of Strassen's algorithm at 62.5% success for correct outputs and improved solution efficiency for other algorithms, even for those not fully reinvented from zero success.
The GRPO-based unlearning method achieved an average forgetting rate between 96.0% and 100.0% while largely preserving the models' general utility on standard benchmarks.
solution: The "Unlearn-and-Reinvent" pipeline was developed, consisting of an unlearning phase to remove specific algorithmic knowledge and a reinvention phase to assess independent discovery.

A Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)-based unlearning method with a multi-dimensional reward function was employed to achieve near-complete forgetting while preserving the LLM's general utility.

The reinvention phase featured an interactive coding environment with a generative verifier providing diagnostic feedback, coupled with hierarchical hint levels and test-time reinforcement learning to aid exploration and optimization.
summary.summary: Researchers from Xiongan AI Institute and Tsinghua University introduce an "Unlearn-and-Reinvent" pipeline to evaluate whether Large Language Models can invent foundational algorithms after explicitly forgetting them. The strongest unlearned model successfully reinvented 50% of ten target algorithms without hints, demonstrating an inherent capacity for algorithmic invention beyond memorization.

title: Can Large Language Models Reinvent Foundational Algorithms?
